description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant "Check for updates" placeholder icon, and the target scientific figure from the research on Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%) low-density steels cannot be loaded correctly. No valid information related to material microstructure, phase composition, mechanical properties, characterization results or other research content of the paper can be extracted from the current visible placeholder. No figure caption or corresponding citation sentences are provided to supplement the content of this figure, so the actual scientific value of the figure cannot be determined.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt% (marked as 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be respectively). The X-axis is diffraction angle 2θ ranging from 20° to 100°, and Y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). Identified phases include γ austenite, δ ferrite, Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe, and BeC, as marked on the patterns. Phase composition evolves with increasing Be content: the 0Be sample shows a single austenite phase, the 0.5Be sample presents austenite with Be-Fe intermetallic precipitates, while 1.0Be and 1.5Be samples form a dual-phase structure of austenite and ferrite with more Be-Fe intermetallic phases. These XRD results confirm the phase regulation effect of Be addition in the studied steel system, consistent with corresponding microstructure characterization results.

This group of optical micrographs presents the as-cast microstructures of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with varying Be contents (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%), each subgraph attached with a 50 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) for 0Be steel shows a single uniform austenite (γ) phase microstructure. Subfigure (b) for 0.5Be steel exhibits dot-like beryllium compounds precipitated on the austenite matrix. Subfigures (c) (1.0Be) and (d) (1.5Be) display dual-phase microstructures composed of austenite (γ), ferrite (δ), and distinct Chinese character-shaped Be-Fe intermetallic compounds. The ferrite grain size in 1.5Be steel is smaller than that in 1.0Be steel, and the volume fraction of Be-Fe phase rises with increasing Be content, consistent with XRD characterization results. This figure validates that Be addition modulates the phase constitution and precipitate morphology of as-cast low-density steels, with Be content exceeding 0.5 wt% inducing ferrite formation and Be-Fe intermetallic precipitation.

This set of figures presents bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–0.5Be low-density steel. Subfigure (a) shows low-magnification microstructure with a 5 μm scale bar, marking two characteristic regions A and B. Subfigure (b) is the magnified view of region A (1 μm scale bar) with the selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) sampling position labeled, and subfigure (c) is the magnified view of region B (1 μm scale bar) with SADP sampling position labeled. Subfigures (d) and (e) are SADPs of the Be₁₂Fe intermetallic precipitate: pattern (d) is indexed along the [-113] zone axis with diffraction spots of (2-5-1), (152), (301) planes marked, and pattern (e) is indexed along the [001] zone axis with (101), (202) planes marked. This figure confirms the existence of Be₁₂Fe precipitates in as-cast 0.5 wt% Be-containing low-density steel, directly supporting the conclusion that excess Be addition forms Be-Fe intermetallic phases in the alloy matrix.

This figure presents bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterizations of as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) display different morphologies of Be₁₂Fe precipitates, both with a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigure (c) shows the morphology of AlFe₃ precipitates (scale bar 1 μm), with a marked position for selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) collection. Subfigure (d) is the corresponding SADP, indexed to the [011] zone axis of the AlFe₃ phase, with diffraction spots corresponding to {111}, {1$ ar{1}$$ ar{1}$}, and {200} planes identified. These results confirm the coexistence of Be₁₂Fe and AlFe₃ intermetallic precipitates in the as-cast 1.5 wt% Be-containing low-density steel, supporting the conclusion that increased Be addition promotes the formation of multiple Fe-based intermetallic precipitates in this alloy system.

This bar chart presents the Vickers hardness (HV) of different phases in as-cast Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels, where x equals 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt% and labeled as 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be respectively. Red bars represent austenite while blue bars represent ferrite. The measured values are: austenite hardness is 453.31 HV for 0Be, 462.88 HV for 0.5Be, 475.80 HV for 1.0Be, and 479.74 HV for 1.5Be; ferrite only forms in 1.0Be and 1.5Be dual-phase steels, with hardness of 437.40 HV and 452.54 HV respectively. Austenite hardness rises continuously with increasing Be content, and ferrite hardness also increases with Be content in dual-phase samples, while ferrite hardness is consistently lower than austenite hardness for the same Be addition. This figure confirms that Be addition effectively improves the matrix hardness of as-cast low-density steels, partially contributed by Be-containing precipitates, and ferrite formed at higher Be content shows lower hardness than austenite.

This figure presents the heating differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves of homogenized Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels, with x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt% corresponding to 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be samples respectively. The x-axis denotes temperature ranging from 1050 °C to 1350 °C, and the y-axis represents DSC signal in mW/mg. Measured phase transition peak temperatures are 1320 °C for 0Be alloy, 1165.9 °C for 0.5Be alloy, 1163.3 °C for 1.0Be alloy, and 1154.8 °C for 1.5Be alloy. It is observed that Be addition significantly reduces the phase transition temperature by approximately 120 °C compared to Be-free steel, and the peak temperature gradually decreases with increasing Be content, confirming that Be addition effectively regulates the phase transformation behavior of the studied low-density steel system.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels after hot rolling at 1050 °C, with Be content x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt% (labeled as 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be respectively). The x-axis is diffraction angle 2θ ranging from 20° to 100°, and the y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary unit (a.u.). Four phases are identified with corresponding markers: γ-austenite (FCC structure, red heart), δ-ferrite (BCC structure, blue diamond), Be11Fe (orange downward triangle), and Be2Fe (green star). No ferrite or Be-Fe intermetallic phases are detected in 0Be and 0.5Be samples; δ-ferrite and Be11Fe phase appear when Be content reaches 1.0 wt%, and all four phases are observed in 1.5Be sample. These results verify that Be solubility in austenite is below 1 wt%, and excess Be promotes ferrite formation and precipitates as Be-Fe compounds, supporting the regulation effect of Be content on phase composition of the tested steels.

This figure presents optical microscopy (OM) morphologies of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steel strips after hot rolling at 1050 °C, with four subgraphs corresponding to different Be contents: (a) x=0 wt% (0Be), (b) x=0.5 wt% (0.5Be), (c) x=1.0 wt% (1.0Be), (d) x=1.5 wt% (1.5Be), all equipped with a 50 μm scale bar. For 0Be steel (a), the microstructure is single γ-Fe (austenite) phase with annealing twins and limited precipitates. 0.5Be steel (b) retains single austenite phase, with dispersed dot-like Be-Fe precipitates, coarser grains, and visible annealing twins. When Be content increases to 1.0 wt% (c) and 1.5 wt% (d), the microstructure transforms to dual-phase structure consisting of γ-Fe and α-Fe (ferrite), both phases are elongated along the rolling direction, while the content of dispersed Be-Fe precipitates rises significantly, and annealing twins are still observed in austenite. This figure reveals that increasing Be content regulates the phase constitution, precipitation behavior, and microstructure morphology of hot-rolled low-density steels, verifying the modification effect of Be addition on steel microstructure.

This figure presents SEM morphology and corresponding EDS elemental mapping of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–1.5Be low-density steel strips after hot rolling at 1050 °C. Subfigure (a) shows the junction region of austenite and ferrite with a 1 μm scale bar, where region A marks the elongated strip-shaped phase. Subfigures (b) to (e) display elemental distribution maps of Fe, Mn, C, and Al respectively at the same magnification with 1 μm scale bars. EDS results confirm that strip-shaped region A is enriched in Mn and Al, while Fe shows relatively uniform distribution and C has no obvious segregation. This evidence verifies that the elongated structure is ferrite phase surrounded by austenite matrix, supporting the dual-phase structure formation of 1.5Be-bearing low-density steel after hot rolling.

This figure presents tensile mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%) low-density steels after hot rolling at 1050 °C, consisting of two subplots. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves (y-axis: engineering stress in MPa, x-axis: engineering strain in %), with a schematic of the tensile test specimen attached. Curves for four samples (0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be) indicate that 0.5Be achieves the highest total elongation (~40.47%), 0Be and 1.5Be exhibit higher ultimate tensile strength (~1222 MPa and ~1201 MPa respectively), while 1.0Be has the lowest yield strength (~820 MPa). Subplot (b) displays strain hardening rate (dσ/dε, unit GPa) as a function of true strain (%), showing that all samples undergo rapid initial drop in strain hardening rate, followed by a steady-state stage where hardening rate rises with increasing Be content, and 0.5Be has the lowest steady-state hardening rate. The figure supports that 0.5 wt% Be addition delivers the optimal strength-ductility combination, with Be content regulating mechanical performance via phase transition (single austenite to austenite-ferrite dual phase) and precipitate evolution.

This bar chart presents the Vickers hardness (unit: HV) of austenite matrix in hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels, with x (Be content) set as 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt% (samples labeled as 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be respectively). The measured austenite hardness values are 415.55 HV for 0Be sample, 470.97 HV for 0.5Be sample, 398.39 HV for 1.0Be sample, and 407.01 HV for 1.5Be sample. The hardness trend shows a first increase to the peak at 0.5 wt% Be addition, then decreases to the minimum at 1.0 wt% Be, followed by a slight rise at 1.5 wt% Be. It demonstrates that 0.5 wt% Be addition effectively enhances the austenite matrix hardness of the hot-rolled test steels, which correlates with dispersed Be-containing precipitates and increased dislocation density induced by moderate Be addition.

This figure presents SEM tensile fracture morphologies of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with different Be contents: (a) x=0 (0Be steel), (b) x=0.5 wt% (0.5Be steel), (c) x=1.0 wt% (1.0Be steel), (d) x=1.5 wt% (1.5Be steel). The inset in each subgraph shows the macroscopic fracture morphology, with the marked area corresponding to the magnified SEM view. 0Be and 0.5Be steels exhibit typical ductile fracture features dominated by dimples, with few cleavage planes observed. As Be content increases to 1.0 wt% and 1.5 wt%, the area of cleavage surfaces at the fracture increases gradually, and obvious cleavage steps are visible in the 1.5Be sample, indicating the fracture mode transforms from fully ductile fracture to ductile-brittle mixed fracture with increasing Be addition. This observation correlates with the decreased elongation of 1.0Be and 1.5Be steels, and supports the conclusion that excessive Be addition introduces brittle Be-rich precipitates and degrades tensile ductility of the test steels.

This figure presents initial electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, denoted as 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be respectively) low-density steels after hot rolling at 1050 °C. It includes 4 columns of subgraphs for each sample with varied Be content: the first column (a1/b1/c1/d1) are image quality (IQ) maps marking grain boundaries of different misorientation angles; the second column (a2/b2/c2/d2) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps showing grain orientation; the third column (a3/b3/c3/d3) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps characterizing local strain and stress concentration; the fourth column (a4/b4/c4/d4) are phase distribution maps where green represents austenite and red represents ferrite. The scale bar is 100 μm for 0Be and 0.5Be samples, and 60 μm for 1.0Be and 1.5Be samples. Obvious trends are observed: ferrite content increases gradually with rising Be content, KAM maps show apparent stress concentration at precipitation sites of carbides and Be-Fe phases, while stress distribution is relatively uniform in high-Be (1.0Be, 1.5Be) samples. This figure supports the conclusion that Be addition regulates phase composition, precipitation behavior and micro-stress distribution of the low-density steels.

This group of bar charts presents grain size area fraction distributions of Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%) low-density steels, characterized via EBSD analysis. Subplot (a) corresponds to 0Be steel, with grain size peaking at ~15 μm; subplot (b) corresponds to 0.5Be steel, showing significantly coarser grains peaking at ~40 μm; subplot (c) corresponds to 1.0Be steel, with grain size peaking at ~10 μm; subplot (d) corresponds to 1.5Be steel, with grain size peaking at ~12 μm. The trend shows that adding 0.5 wt% Be leads to obvious grain coarsening, while further increasing Be content to 1.0 and 1.5 wt% refines the grains, which is associated with the grain boundary pinning effect of Be-induced precipitates and ferrite formation. This figure supports that Be content can effectively regulate the grain size of the experimental low-density steels.

This figure presents bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels with x = 0 (0Be), 0.5 (0.5Be), 1.0 (1.0Be), and 1.5 (1.5Be). Subfigures a₁, b₁, c₁, d₁ show the overall microstructures of 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be steels respectively. Subfigures a₂, b₂, d₂ are SAED patterns of austenite (γ-Fe) in 0Be, 0.5Be, 1.5Be steels, collected along [1̅14], [1̅11], [013] zone axes respectively. Subfigures a₃, b₃, c₃, d₃ exhibit the morphology of carbide precipitates in the four steels. Subfigures a₄ to d₄ are SAED patterns of the carbides: κ-carbide (zone axis [001]) is detected in 0Be steel, while Be2C (zone axes [1̅12], [011], [1̅12] for 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be steels respectively) is identified in Be-containing steels. The results confirm that Be addition transforms the carbide precipitates from κ-carbide to Be2C in hot-rolled low-density steels, supporting the Be-induced phase evolution mechanism.

This set of bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images displays the microstructures of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels, with a uniform 2 μm scale bar for each subfigure. Subfigure (a) presents the structure of 0.5 wt% Be steel, (b) presents the structure of 1.0 wt% Be steel, and (c) presents the structure of 1.5 wt% Be steel. White arrows in each subfigure point to dislocations in the matrix. A clear trend is observed: the dislocation density of the rolled steel gradually increases with the increase of Be content, which is consistent with the EBSD KAM statistical results. This figure supports the core conclusion that the addition of Be elevates the dislocation density in the low-density steel, thus realizing the strengthening effect on the material.

This schematic diagram illustrates the microstructure evolution of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels as Be content increases. The four subplots correspond to samples with 0 wt% Be (a), 0.5 wt% Be (b), 1.0 wt% Be (c), and 1.5 wt% Be (d), respectively. All samples contain γ austenite matrix with annealing twins. As Be content rises: the 0Be sample has single austenite phase without extra precipitates; the 0.5Be sample forms dispersed orange precipitated phases in austenite grains and at grain boundaries; the 1.0Be sample presents green α ferrite phases in austenite matrix, with further increased amount of precipitates; the 1.5Be sample shows more abundant precipitates distributed both in austenite, ferrite grains and at boundaries, with ferrite containing embedded precipitates. This figure demonstrates that increasing Be addition first promotes precipitation of second phases, then induces ferrite formation when Be content exceeds 1.0 wt%, which supports the regulation mechanism of Be on phase composition and microstructure of the studied low-density steels.

This set of three schematic crystal structure diagrams illustrates solid solution types of different solute atoms in the Fe matrix of the investigated Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe low-density steels. The first subplot shows a smaller Be atom as substitutional solute, attracting surrounding Fe atoms and causing lattice shrinkage distortion. The second subplot shows a larger Al atom as substitutional solute, pushing away surrounding Fe atoms and leading to lattice expansion distortion. The third subplot shows small C atoms occupying interstitial sites of the Fe lattice as interstitial solutes. The figure demonstrates that different atomic radius differences between solutes and Fe cause varied lattice distortion energy, which affects solid solubility, phase formation, and density regulation of the steels: Al-induced lattice expansion effectively reduces steel density while Be-induced shrinkage contributes little to density reduction, excess Be exceeding solubility forms Be-Fe intermetallic phases, and lattice distortion also expels C from interstitial sites to form carbide precipitates. It supports the solid solution strengthening and phase evolution mechanism of Be-containing low-density steels.

This is a thermodynamic equilibrium phase diagram of the binary Fe-Be system, with the horizontal axis representing the molar ratio of Fe/(Be+Fe) ranging from 0 to 1, and the vertical axis representing temperature ranging from 0 °C to 1800 °C. Identified phase regions include the Liquid phase, solid solution phases (BCC-A2, FCC-A1, HCP-A3), and intermetallic compound phases (FeBe₁₂, FeBe₅, FeBe₂). This phase diagram is applied to interpret phase evolution in Be-added Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C low-density steels: the solubility of Be in austenite is less than 1 wt%, excess Be beyond the solubility limit precipitates as Fe-Be intermetallic compounds or promotes ferrite formation, which matches the experimentally observed microstructure evolution trends in the studied low-density steel series.

This scatter plot compares the product of strength and ductility (y-axis, unit: GPa·%) against density (x-axis, unit: g/cm³) for the developed Fe-30Mn-10Al-1.5C-xBe (x=0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%, denoted as Be steels) and multiple conventional structural steels, including GCr15, 40Cr, Q235, 1Cr23Ni18, other Fe-Mn-Al-C series steels, etc. The developed Be steels are clustered in the low-density region of 6.3~6.5 g/cm³, which is 21.38% lower than pure iron density, while their strength-ductility product ranges from 22 GPa·% to 46 GPa·%, far outperforming most materials at similar density, and also superior to the majority of conventional higher-density steels whose strength-ductility product mostly falls between 5 GPa·% and 33 GPa·%. This figure demonstrates that the developed Be-containing low-density steels achieve an excellent combination of low density and superior comprehensive mechanical properties, defining a new performance space for low-density high-strength steel systems.
